messages.content: What is the professional role and affiliation of the author mentioned in the document?
The author is an Associate Professor of Neuroanatomy & Neurosurgery at the Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, Department of Clinical Medicine, Macquarie University.
According to the text, what makes learning brain anatomy easier?

Understanding the direct relationship of each anatomic structure to its specific neurologic (dys)function, and the description of surgical approaches for three-dimensional understanding of topographic anatomy.
What is the benefit of using eponyms in learning anatomy, as mentioned in the text?
Eponyms help to understand and learn anatomy by offering an interesting continuum between history, research, and medicine in the practical clinical setting.
What facilities does the Institute of Anatomy of the Medical University of Vienna offer for learning anatomy?

Students can perform dissections on human bodies in the same rooms where famous anatomists worked, and they have access to innovative technologies like ultra-high-field (7 Tesla) magnetic resonance and tractography for in vivo visualization.
How does Austria's legal and cultural background support anatomical studies?
The laws and cultural background allow for such studies thanks to the generous contribution of many people who voluntarily donate their body to the institute for didactic and research purposes.
What is the primary purpose of the booklet described in the text?

To assist students in understanding brain anatomy and physiology during the short dissection period and in the course 'Brain Anatomy in neurosurgical perspective', contextualizing brain anatomy with clinical remarks.
Why does the text say it may appear 'disbalanced' in some parts?

Because only the most interesting points (as concluded by discussion with students) are presented, and some details like all the nuclei of the cranial nerves in the brain stem have been intentionally skipped.
What language is used in the booklet, and why?

English is used as it is the most widely used language in scientific literature, but anatomical terms are written in Latin according to the 'Terminologia Anatomica' (1998), as is the practice at the Medical University of Vienna.
What is the 'secret' aim of the booklet?
To inject enthusiasm into students to approach the knowledge of the brain and possibly ignite passion to deepen the topics and contribute to neuroscience in the future.
What specific contributions did Prof. Dr. Manfred Tschabitscher make to Antonio Di Ieva's career?

Prof. Dr. Manfred Tschabitscher was Antonio Di Ieva's Maestro in the history of medicine, German teacher, mentor in anatomical research, supervisor of his PhD project in Clinical Neurosciences, and gave him the chance to teach neuroanatomy to medical students.
Who has used the booklet besides undergraduate and medical students?
Neurosurgery residents and specialists at the Medical University of Vienna and the University of Toronto have also used the booklet.
Why does the author contextualize topographic anatomy in surgical applications?
The author contextualizes topographic anatomy in surgical applications because over time it has been confirmed that this makes the 'arid' anatomy more interesting.
Why did the Egyptians remove the brain during mummification?
The brain was initially considered an organ of secondary importance, so Egyptians removed it from cadavers via the nose and threw it away, while other organs were preserved in canopic jars.
What did Aristotle believe the function of the brain was?
For Aristotle, the brain was a mere 'cooling structure', and the organs considered most important were the liver and the heart.
Why did Galenic theories lead to erroneous descriptions of human anatomy?
Galen performed dissections only on animals, and the arbitrary transposition of his observations to humans led to erroneous descriptions of human anatomy.
What milestone did Guido da Vigevano introduce in anatomy?
Guido da Vigevano introduced the use of anatomical illustrations, creating a milestone in the development of anatomy as a scientific and artistic field.
During which period did anatomy fully develop in its modern meaning, with the birth of anatomical theaters?
The exploit of anatomy in its modern meaning occurred during the Renaissance, with the birth of many anatomical theaters, such as the famous theater in Padua.
What did Leonardo da Vinci's search for the relationship between structure and function lead to?
The search for the relationship between structure and function, as postulated by Leonardo, gave rise to the trend of looking for the 'seat of the soul' in the subsequent centuries.
Which structures were postulated as the seat of the soul over the centuries?
The soul was postulated to be seated in the corpus callosum, the ventricles, the cerebrospinal fluid, the pineal gland, or the cortex.
Who provided the first accurate descriptions of the cerebral lobes and fissures, especially the optic radiations?
Louis Pierre Gratiolet (1815-1865) provided the first accurate descriptions of the cerebral lobes and fissures, especially of the optic radiations.
What is the cerebral falx and where is it located?

The cerebral falx is the portion of dura mater running in the depth of the longitudinal fissure, separating the two hemispheres, except in its more anterior part where the medial faces of the frontal lobes (cingulate gyri) face each other.
What are the two components of the neurocranium?
The neurocranium has two components: the 'hub' (calvaria) and the floor of the cranial cavity, which is defined as the skull base.
How will the clinico-anatomical relationship between the foramina of the skull base and the cranial nerves be described?
The clinico-anatomical relationship between the foramina of the skull base and the cranial nerves will be immediately described in the following section.
What bones primarily form the cranial vault?
The cranial vault is primarily formed by the frontal, parietal, occipital, and temporal bones, with contributions from the greater wing of the sphenoid bone.
How do the lobes of the brain generally get their names?
The lobes of the brain generally take their name from the overlying bones, with the exception of the insula, which is buried in the lateral sulcus and has no direct relationship with the bone.
What is the function of the holes present on the calvaria?
The holes on the calvaria are generally crossed by anastomotic veins between the meninges and the epicranic veins, for example, the parietal foramen.
What is the definition of craniosynostosis according to the text?

Craniosynostosis is defined as premature ossification of the interdigitations of the cranial sutures, causing head deformation and potentially correlating with brain development dysfunctions in some genetic syndromes.
Which bones form the skull base?

The bones that form the skull base are: frontal bones, sphenoid bone, ethmoid bone, temporal bones, and occipital bone. The anterior part of the exocranial surface is also formed by the maxillary and palate bones.
How is the skull base anatomically divided?

The skull base is anatomically divided into three parts (fossae): the anterior skull base (anterior cranial fossa), the middle skull base (middle cranial fossa), and the posterior skull base (posterior cranial fossa).
What are the boundaries of the anterior skull base?
The anterior skull base is delimited anteriorly by the frontal bone and the posterior wall of the frontal sinus, and posteriorly by the limen of the lesser wing of sphenoid bone.
What forms the middle skull base?
The middle skull base is formed by the sphenoid bone and temporal bones. It is delimited anteriorly by the lesser wings of sphenoid bones and posteriorly by the pars petrosa of the temporal bones.
What is the central part of the middle cranial fossa defined as?
The central part of the middle cranial fossa is defined as the sella turcica, which is part of the sphenoid bone.
What are the components and relationships of the sella turcica?

The sellar compartment contains the hypophysis, separated by the 'suprasellar' compartment (brain) from the diaphragma sellae, which has an opening at the center (the ostium of the diaphragma sellae) crossed by the pituitary stalk. The lateral parts are the 'parasellar compartments', location of the cavernous sinus, crossed by some cranial nerves and the internal carotid artery.
Why is the sella turcica strategically important for neurosurgeons?
It is the main 'door' to access the pituitary gland and brain via endonasal approaches (endonasal transsphenoidal approaches, performed with the aid of the operative microscope or endoscopically).
What forms the posterior skull base?

The posterior skull base is formed by the occipital bone. It is delimited anteriorly by the posterior walls of the pars petrosa of the temporal bone and posteriorly by the grooves for the transverse sinuses. It contains the foramen magnum, crossed by the brain stem which continues downward into the spinal cord.
Name some exocranial structures above the skull base.
Specific structures include the pterygoid process (medial and lateral plates), the styloid process of the temporal bone, the tip of the mastoid, and the occipital condyles.
Where are the frontal sinuses located?

The cavities of the frontal sinus are situated in the frontal bone, behind the supraorbital margin, delimited by anterior and posterior walls, and are very heterogeneous in shape, volume, and presence/morphology of septae.
What is the sphenoid sinus and its significance?

The sphenoid sinus is contained within the body of the sphenoid bone and is very heterogeneous in shape, volume, and presence/morphology of septae. It is the main 'door' for the endonasal transsphenoidal approach.
What are the two branches of the oculomotor nerve in the orbit?

A superior branch (for superior rectus and levator palpebrae) and an inferior branch (for medial rectus, inferior rectus, and oblique inferior muscles, plus parasympathetic fibers for the ciliary ganglion).
What is the origin of the accessory nerve (XI) and which foramina does it pass through?
The accessory nerve is formed by axons from the upper cervical spinal cord. It ascends into the skull through the foramen magnum and leaves the skull through the jugular foramen.
Which muscles does the accessory nerve (XI) innervate, and what are the effects of its lesion?
The accessory nerve innervates the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles. Lesions result in shoulder drop, downward and lateral rotation of the scapula, and weakness in head rotation.
Where does the hypoglossal nerve (XII) emerge from, and through which foramen does it exit the skull?
The hypoglossal nerve emerges from the ventrolateral sulcus of the medulla oblongata in several rootlets and exits the skull through the hypoglossal foramen, anterior to the occipital condyle.
Which tongue muscle is not innervated by the hypoglossal nerve (XII), and what happens in a peripheral nerve lesion?

The palatoglossus muscle is innervated by the vagus nerve, not the hypoglossal nerve. In a peripheral hypoglossal nerve lesion, the tongue deviates toward the side of the inactive muscle due to the action of the contralateral functioning muscles.
How many layers do the meninges consist of, and what are they called?
The meninges consist of three layers: the pia mater (adherent to the cortex), the arachnoid mater (intermediate), and the dura mater (the most external and thick layer).
What are cisterns in the brain?

Cisterns are areas of subarachnoid space, always filled with cerebrospinal fluid, and frequently contain neurovascular structures (blood vessels and nerves). They are larger cavities below the arachnoid.
What is the most significant artery that vascularizes the meninges, and where does it originate?
The middle meningeal artery is the most significant. It comes from the maxillary artery and enters the middle cranial fossa via the foramen spinosum.
What is an epidural hematoma and what causes it?

An epidural hematoma is an accumulation of arterial blood (under high pressure) in the epidural space, often caused by a fracture of the calvaria or skull base along the course of the middle meningeal artery, leading to laceration of the artery.
What is the mechanism behind contralateral weakness in an epidural hematoma?

The compressed hemisphere causes herniation of the uncus into the tentorial incisura, compressing the ipsilateral cerebral peduncle, which carries the pyramidal tract for contralateral muscle innervation. Its dislocation causes contralateral hemiparesis.
What is the mechanism behind ipsilateral mydriasis in an epidural hematoma?

The dislocation of the uncus compresses the oculomotor nerve (third nerve), which runs below the tentorium. The weak parasympathetic fibers from the Edinger-Westphal nucleus, responsible for pupillary constriction, are disrupted, causing mydriasis.
What is the Kernohan phenomenon?

The Kernohan phenomenon is a rare neurological condition where ipsilateral mydriasis in an epidural hematoma is associated with ipsilateral weakness (instead of contralateral), due to excessive herniation causing compression of the contralateral cerebral peduncle against the tentorial free margin.
What is a subdural hematoma and how does it occur?

A subdural hematoma is an accumulation of blood below the dura mater (classically between arachnoid and dura, but histologically intradural). It occurs due to stretching and rupture of bridging veins, especially in older patients with brain atrophy, even after mild head trauma.
What is a subarachnoid hematoma and what causes it?

A subarachnoid hematoma is an accumulation of blood between the pia mater and arachnoid, generally caused by rupture of arteries running in the subarachnoid space due to head trauma or congenital diseases like aneurysms.
Describe the structure and location of the cerebral falx.

The cerebral falx is a fold of dura mater descending vertically in the longitudinal fissure, dividing the cranial cavity into right and left symmetric cavities. It attaches rostrally to the internal cranial surface (containing the superior sagittal sinus in its triangular-shaped fold), has a free caudal margin containing the inferior sagittal sinus, and attaches perpendicularly to the cerebellar tentorium where the straight sinus runs.
What is the cerebellar tentorium and its function?

The cerebellar tentorium is a meningeal 'tent' covering the cerebellum. It attaches anteriorly to the pars petrosa of the temporal bone (superior petrosal sinus) and posteriorly to the internal surfaces of the occipital bone (transverse sinus). It has a central anterior opening (tentorial incisura) for the brain stem, dividing the cranial cavity into supratentorial and infratentorial compartments.
What is the diaphragma sellae?

The diaphragma sellae is a horizontal meningeal fold attaching to the four clinoid processes, forming the roof of the sella turcica. It has an opening (ostium) crossed by the pituitary stalk, separating the supradiaphragmatic space from the infradiaphragmatic space where the pituitary gland lies.
What are the ventricles of the brain and how many are there?
The ventricles are cavities in the brain filled with cerebrospinal fluid. There are two lateral ventricles (C-shaped in the hemispheres), the third ventricle, and the fourth ventricle.
How does CSF flow from the lateral ventricles to the fourth ventricle?
CSF flows from the lateral ventricles to the third ventricle via the interventricular foramen (foramen of Monro), and from the third to the fourth ventricle via the aqueduct of Sylvius.
Through which openings does CSF exit the fourth ventricle into the subarachnoid space?
CSF exits the fourth ventricle via three openings: the median opening (foramen of Magendie) and the two lateral openings (foramina of Luschka).
What is the normal volume of CSF in adults and how fast is it turned over?
The normal volume of CSF in adults is about 150-200 ml, and the whole amount is turned over about three times per 24 hours.
Where is the largest cistern in the human body located?
The largest cistern is the lumbar cistern, located in the spine at the level between vertebrae L2 and S2, where spinal tap is generally performed.
Why are cisterns important for neurosurgeons?

Cisterns are important because they are often filled by pathological conditions (e.g., tumors and aneurysms) and serve as surgical corridors to reach deep parts of the brain, avoiding the intraparenchymal route.
How many brain cisterns have been described since their first description by Key and Retzius?
More than 20 brain cisterns have been described in detail since their first description by Key and Retzius in 1875.
Which cisterns are unpaired and which are paired?
Cisterns located on the midline are unpaired structures, while lateral cisterns (e.g., sylvian cistern and ambient cistern) are paired structures (left and right).
What is the relationship of the chiasmatic cistern with other structures?

It is in close relationship with the interpeduncular cistern posteriorly (via Liliequist's membrane), with the two lateral cisterns containing the carotid arteries, with the sylvian cisterns, and with the infundibulum. It is located over the diaphragma sellae.
What is the interpeduncular cistern and what forms its anterior wall?

The interpeduncular cistern occupies the interpeduncular fossa, where the two cerebral peduncles converge to the pons. Its anterior wall is the Liliequist's membrane, a thick arachnoidal membrane stretching between the mesial surfaces of the temporal poles.
What structures does the interpeduncular cistern contain?
It contains the top of the basilar artery with its quadrifurcation (two posterior cerebral arteries and two superior cerebellar arteries) and the oculomotor nerve.
What is the ambient cistern?
The ambient cistern is located laterally to the mesencephalon at the level of the tentorial incisura, crossing both the supra- and infratentorial spaces.
Which structures cross the ambient cistern?
The main structures crossing the ambient cistern are the posterior cerebral artery, the basal vein (of Rosenthal), the trochlear nerve, the superior cerebellar artery, and the optic tracts.
What is the epidural space?
The epidural space is a virtual space between the periosteal layer and the dura mater, which are normally fused. It can be filled by pathological conditions (e.g., meningeal tumors or bleeding).
What is the consequence of a midline shift due to an epidural hematoma?
A midline shift compresses the underlying ipsilateral hemisphere, shifting structures to the contralateral side, causing contralateral weakness and ipsilateral mydriasis.

What is the role of the pineal gland? (Note: not explicitly mentioned, but one question can be derived from context? Actually no, skip or rephrase) Actually, the content does not mention the pineal gland. Revise: What is the function of the Liliequist's membrane?
Liliequist's membrane is a thick arachnoidal membrane that forms the anterior wall of the interpeduncular cistern, stretching between the mesial surfaces of the temporal poles.
Where does the CSF get reabsorbed?
The greater portion of CSF is reabsorbed by capillaries in the subarachnoid space of the spine, not by the granulationes Pacchionii as previously believed.
What is the foramen of Magendie?
The foramen of Magendie is the median opening of the fourth ventricle through which CSF passes into the cisterns of the posterior cranial fossa.
What is the foramen of Luschka?
The foramen of Luschka is one of the two lateral openings of the fourth ventricle through which CSF passes into the cisterns of the posterior cranial fossa.
What is the largest cistern in the intracranial cavity and what is its clinical relevance?

The dorsal cerebellomedullary cistern (cisterna magna) is the largest cistern. It can be reached via a suboccipital puncture for CSF sampling, but this hazardous technique no longer has clinical application.
What structures are contained within the cisterna magna?
The cisterna magna contains the lower medulla, the lower cerebellar vermis, the lower parts of the two cerebellar hemispheres, the posterior inferior cerebellar arteries, and vertebral arteries.
What is the phylogenetic classification of the neocortex compared to the paleocortex and archicortex?
The neocortex is the most recent development of the telencephalon, different from the paleocortex (e.g., olfactory cortex) and from the archicortex (hippocampal cortex).
What are the six typical layers of the neocortex, ordered from the surface to the deeper layers?

The six typical layers of the neocortex are: I - Molecular layer, II - External granular layer, III - External pyramidal layer, IV - Internal granular layer, V - Internal pyramidal layer, and VI - Multiform layer.
What are 'Betz giant pyramidal cells' and where are they located?

Betz giant pyramidal cells are a special kind of large pyramidal cells located in lamina V (the internal pyramidal layer). They are the first motoneurons of the pyramidal tract, giving rise to the axons forming the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts.
What is Gennari's stria and which part of the cortex is it associated with?
Gennari's stria is the external stria of the calcarine cortex, visible to the naked eye, and it is part of the visual cortex.
How is the cortex defined in terms of 'agranular' and 'granular' based on layer distribution?

The cortex is defined as 'agranular' when there is a predominance of pyramidal cells, for example in the motor area, and 'granular' when there is an abundance of granular cells, such as in the somatosensory areas.
What anatomical and physiological peculiarities can be observed between the two hemispheres of the same brain?
The two hemispheres show some anatomical and physiological peculiarities, such as the volumetric asymmetry of the speech-related areas, which are bigger in the dominant hemisphere.
Why is it necessary to split the frontal from the temporal lobe to reach the insula?
The insula is 'buried' in the depth of the lateral fissure, making it necessary to split the frontal from the temporal lobe to reach it.
How does the classic anatomic terminology of the four cerebral lobes relate to the overlying cranial bone?

The classic anatomic terminology of the four cerebral lobes is related to the name of the overlying cranial bone (the 'fifth' lobe, the insula, is instead 'buried' in the depth of the lateral fissure).
Which meninges are removed to visualize the gyri and sulci, and which one is too thin to be removed?
The dura mater and arachnoid are removed, while the pia mater is too thin and adherent to the glial membrane to be removed from the cortex.
What is the role of identifying the most important sulci?
Identifying the most important sulci is essential to delineate the borders of the cerebral lobes, as well as important anatomical landmarks and functional regions.
Describe the location and direction of the lateral sulcus.
The lateral sulcus runs in an oblique direction, separating the temporal lobe below from the frontal lobe and parietal lobe above.
What does the lateral sulcus overlie?
It overlies the sylvian fissure, a deep cleft by means of which it is possible to reach the most deep and hidden parts of the brain.
How can the central sulcus be recognized?

It can be recognized by first identifying the precentral sulcus (the first vertical continuous sulcus on the convexity), then the central sulcus posterior to it, and more posteriorly, the postcentral sulcus.
What is the 'plique de passage'?
It is a bridge of cortex between the pre- and postcentral gyri, left because the central sulcus (generally) does not reach the lateral sulcus, ending about 2 cm before it.
What are the directions and shape of the central sulcus?
The central sulcus runs postero-anteriorly with an oblique direction of about 70° in regard to the lateral sulcus, having the shape of an inverted italic 'S'.
Which sulcus helps identify the borders of the parietal and occipital lobes?

The parieto-occipital sulcus helps identify these borders: the parietal lobe lies between the central sulcus and the parieto-occipital sulcus, and the occipital lobe lies posteriorly to the parieto-occipital sulcus.
How can the insula be visualized?

It requires a gentle supero-inferior splitting of the lateral sulcus to separate the frontal lobe from the temporal lobe, revealing the insula with its typical triangular shape converging in its apex, the limen insulae.
What are the three parts into which the inferior frontal gyrus is divided?

The three parts are the pars orbitalis (below the horizontal ramus), pars triangularis (between the two rami), and pars opercularis (posterior to the vertical ramus and anterior to the precentral sulcus).
What is the clinical relevance of identifying the parts of the inferior frontal gyrus?

The apex of the three parts indicates the access point to the sylvian fissure, where neurosurgeons split the arachnoid to separate the frontal lobe from the temporal lobe for access to the insula and lateral cistern.
What is the result of a lesion in Broca's area in the dominant hemisphere?
Lesions in Broca's area can result in 'expressive Broca's aphasia', where the patient understands words but cannot articulate them, leading to dysarthric speech.
What is the primary function of the precentral gyrus?
The precentral gyrus, also called the 'primary motor cortex', is involved in the motor function of the contralateral side of the body.
Which part of the motor cortex controls the contralateral hand?
The part of the precentral gyrus with a knob-like structure (shaped like an 'omega' or 'epsilon' in the axial plane) controls the motility of the contralateral hand.
What functions are associated with the paracentral lobule?
The paracentral lobule is involved in sphincter control (voluntary control of bladder function) and the motility of the contralateral foot.
What is the 'frontal lobe syndrome' and what causes it?
Frontal lobe syndrome is caused by bilateral lesions of the frontal lobes and consists of abulia (lack of motivation) and serious personality disturbances.
Which gyri form the inferior parietal lobule?

The inferior parietal lobule is formed by the supramarginal gyrus (upon the ending part of the ramus posterior of the lateral sulcus) and the angular gyrus (upon the ending ramus of the superior temporal sulcus).
What is Gerstmann syndrome, and which areas of the brain are involved?

Gerstmann syndrome is a syndrome related to dysfunction of the angular and supramarginal gyri of the dominant hemisphere in the parietal lobe, characterized by anomia, alexia, acalculia, agraphia, finger agnosia, and inability to coordinate the left side with the right side.
Which temporal gyri contain the transverse temporal gyri (of Heschl) and what is their function?
The posterior part of the superior temporal gyrus contains the transverse temporal gyri (of Heschl), which include the terminal axons of the auditory cortex.
What type of condition may result from lesions to the auditory cortex (areas 41 and 42 of Brodmann), as mentioned in the content?
Lesions to the auditory cortex may affect hearing (though unilateral lesions have little effect) or may cause auditory aphasia, where the patient can hear but not understand.
What specific signs are associated with lesions of the occipital lobe?

Specific signs include central blindness, central color blindness (achromatopsia), selective difficulty to identify faces (prosapognosia), and dysfunction in perceiving movements (cortical akinetopsia).
What type of cortex forms the insula, and how is it classified relative to neocortex and archicortex?
The insula is formed by mesocortex, which is a transitional cortex between the neocortex and the archicortex of the limbic system.
How does the central sulcus of the insula relate to the central sulcus of the brain, and what does it divide?
The central sulcus of the insula is the ideal continuation of the central sulcus of the brain (of Rolando), dividing the insula into an anterior and posterior region.
What are the components of the anterior insula and the posterior insula in terms of gyri?
The anterior insula is composed of three short insular gyri. The posterior insula is composed of two long insular gyri.
What is the insular apex, and which gyri converge to form it?
The insular apex is the nearest point of the insula to the brain surface, formed by the insular transverse gyrus and insular accessory gyrus converging to a common point.
Where is the medial surface of the brain located, and which structures can be identified there?

On the medial surface of the brain, one can discern the structures of the limbic lobe as well as the medial projections of the lobes, such as the paracentral lobule (part of the frontal lobe and parietal lobe), precuneus and cuneus (part of the occipital lobe).
What was the original anatomical definition of the limbic lobe by Broca?

The original definition of the limbic lobe by Broca was anatomically based: it was defined as the arciform convolution surrounding the interhemispheric commissures and the upper brain stem, formed by two convolutions, the cingulate gyrus and the parahippocampal gyrus.
Why was the limbic lobe originally named the rhinencephalon, and what is its modern understanding?

It was named the rhinencephalon because it was thought to be involved in the mechanisms of olfaction, even though it is now known that it is more involved in psychic and memory functions (Papez's circuit).
How is the limbic lobe described in terms of evolutionary brain development?

The limbic lobe (better defined as the 'limbic system') is a kind of transitional zone between the most phylogenetically recent brain (neocortex) and the oldest brain (archicortex), and is essentially formed by a cortical structure defined as the mesocortex.
Where is the belt of the limbic lobe situated, and what structures form the limbic system?

The belt of the limbic lobe is situated beneath the corpus callosum. The limbic system is formed by the hippocampal formation and amygdala, septum pellucidum, hypothalamus, as well as the central olfactory system which has diffuse connections with limbic and paralimbic structures.
From an embryological viewpoint, how does the hippocampus develop in relation to the corpus callosum?
Embryologically, the hippocampus develops around the corpus callosum in three parts, with the 'true hippocampus' being the retrocommissural (below the corpus callosum) portion.
How is the hippocampal gray matter histologically distinguished from the neocortex?
The hippocampal gray matter has a laminar differentiation (archicortex), and the three-layer hippocampal cortex is separated from the six-layer neocortex by a transitional mesocortex.
What are the cytoarchitectonic fields of the cornu Ammonis and their sensitivity to hypoxia?
The cornu Ammonis is divided into three fields; CA1 is called the 'vulnerable sector' due to its sensitivity to hypoxia, while CA3 is the 'resistant sector'.
What is the margo denticulatus and where is it visible?
The margo denticulatus is a narrow segment of the dentate gyrus, visible on the temporal surface, with a characteristic toothed appearance.
What are the three segments of the hippocampus and their approximate total length?
The hippocampus has three segments: the head (anterior), body (middle), and tail (posterior), with a total length of about 4 cm.
What covers the intraventricular side of the hippocampus except the head?
The choroid plexus, attached to the taenia choroidea and taenia fornicis, covers the whole intraventricular side of the hippocampus except the head.
What is the uncus and how is it separated from the parahippocampal gyrus and anterior perforated substance?
The uncus is the most medial part of the temporal lobe, separated from the parahippocampal gyrus by the uncal sulcus and from the anterior perforated substance by the rhinal sulcus.
Why is the uncal notch important in intracranial hypertension syndromes?

The uncal notch is caused by the free edge of the tentorium, and this relationship is important for understanding intracranial hypertension syndromes with herniation of the uncus in the tentorial incisura.
What type of epilepsy involves the hippocampus and how often is it refractory to drugs?
Mesial temporal lobe epilepsy involves the hippocampus and amygdala, and it is refractory to antiepileptic drugs in 10% of cases.
What does the neurosurgical operation selective amygdalohippocampectomy involve?

Selective amygdalohippocampectomy involves resection of the anterior part of the hippocampus, lateral part of the amygdala, entire entorhinal cortex, and disconnection of medial temporal structures from the orbito-frontal cortex.
Which arteries supply the hippocampus?
The hippocampus is supplied by vessels deriving from the anterior choroidal artery and the posterior cerebral artery, with its branches.
What are the boundaries of the parahippocampal gyrus?
The parahippocampal gyrus is bounded medially by the uncus, posteriorly by the isthmus of the cingulate gyrus, laterally by the collateral sulcus, and anteriorly by the rhinal sulcus.
What structures does the parahippocampal gyrus envelop, and how are they separated?
The parahippocampal gyrus envelopes the hippocampus, the dentate gyrus, and the cornus ammonis. These structures are separated from the parahippocampal gyrus by the hippocampal sulcus.
What is the shape of the amygdala and where is it located in the temporal lobe?
The amygdala is almond-shaped and located in the dorsomedial portion of the temporal lobe, forming part of the medial and dorsal walls of the inferior horn (temporal horn) of the lateral ventricle.
What is the psalterium and why is it also called Lyra Davidis?
The psalterium is a commissura joining the two columns (crura fornicis) of the fornix. It is also called Lyra Davidis because it resembles a triangular harp.
What is the dorsal part of the septum called and what are its boundaries?
The dorsal part of the septum is called the septum pellucidum. It is a membrane bounded by the basal surface of the corpus callosum and by the rostral convexity of the fornix.
Where is the septum verum located and what does it contain?

The septum verum (the ventral component of the septum) is rostral to the lamina terminalis (the anterior wall of the third ventricle), within the paraterminal gyrus. It contains some nuclei interconnected with the frontal lobe and the limbic system.
What is the name of the area where the basal surface of the frontal lobes lies, and what are its anatomical boundaries?
The basal surface of the frontal lobes is defined as the 'orbital cortex' because it lies on the cribriform plate and roofs of the orbits.
What structures can be visualized in the center of the basal surface of the brain?
In the center of the basal surface, one can visualize the optic system (optic nerves, optic chiasm, and optic tracts), mammillary bodies, tuber cinereum, uncus, and ventral surface of the brain stem.
What is the generic definition of the basal ganglia provided in the content?
The basal ganglia are generically defined as 'gray matter in the central core of the hemispheres', which includes all structures (also white matter) between the insula and the midline of the brain.
Which anatomical structures make up the telencephalic subcortical gray matter according to the content?
The telencephalic subcortical gray matter is organized into distinct anatomical structures: the caudate nucleus, putamen, globus pallidus, claustrum, and some small nuclei.
Why were the putamen and globus pallidus together called the lentiform nucleus?
The putamen and globus pallidus together are lens-shaped on the coronal plane, which is the reason they were called the lentiform nucleus, even though they are different structures.
Which two structures form the dorsal striatum, and what are their main functions?
The putamen and caudate nucleus form the dorsal striatum. They are mainly involved in motor control, as well as in learning, cognition, and memory.
What is the nucleus accumbens and what functions is it involved in?
The nucleus accumbens is a medial expansion of the head of the caudate nucleus. It is involved in reward, pleasure, addiction, fear, and the placebo effect.
What structures form the habenular complex?

The habenular complex is formed of certain gray matter nuclei interconnected with the rhombencephalon (via the fasciculus retroflexus reaching the interpeduncular nucleus) and other structures, serving as a pathway of connection between the telencephalon and brain stem.
Which anatomical structures are involved in the regulation of melatonin production?

The regulation of melatonin production involves a network between the optic nerves, hypothalamus (suprachiasmatic nucleus), the intermediolateral column of the thoracic spinal cord, and the superior cervical ganglion.
Name the four thalamic peduncles and which brain regions they connect.

The superior and posterior thalamic peduncles connect to the parietal lobe and occipital lobe; the inferior thalamic peduncle connects to the temporal, orbito-frontal, insular and amygdaloid structures; the anterior thalamic peduncle connects to the frontal lobe.
What functional role does the thalamus serve?

The thalamus functions as a kind of relay station; sensory relay nuclei are targets of sensory projections to the neocortex, and motor relay nuclei interconnect cerebellar nuclei and extrapyramidal system to motor and pre-motor cortical brain.
What structure divides the mediodorsal thalamic nucleus from the ventral and lateral groups?
The internal medullary lamina, a thin sheet of white matter, divides the mediodorsal thalamic nucleus from the ventral and lateral groups.
What is thalamic pain syndrome?
Thalamic pain syndrome is a lesion of the thalamus resulting in pain which cannot be accurately localized by the patient on his body.
Where is the hypothalamus located within the brain?
The hypothalamus is the ventral part of the diencephalon, located in the lateral walls of the third ventricle, where the hypothalamic sulcus is found.
Why is the red nucleus named 'red'?
The red nucleus is named 'red' because of its color, which is due to the high concentration of iron deposits and its high vascularization.
What are the main inputs and outputs of the red nucleus?

The red nucleus receives inputs from the contralateral cerebellum (mainly via the dentatorubro tract in the superior cerebellar peduncle) and from the ipsilateral motor cortex. It sends outputs mainly to the spine (via the rubrospinal tract) and the cerebellum.
What symptoms are associated with lesions of the pars compacta of the substantia nigra?
Lesions of the pars compacta result in Parkinson's disease, with symptoms including resting tremor, rigidity, slowness, mask-like faces, and shuffling gait.
What is the primary difference between hemiballismus and athetosis as described in the basal ganglia lesion section?
Hemiballismus involves sudden wild movement of one arm, while athetosis involves snake-like movements, especially of the distal parts like fingers and wrists.
What is the purpose of Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS) in functional neurosurgery?
DBS is used to alleviate tremor of various origins by introducing microelectrodes into specific brain targets to neuromodulate their electric function.
What newer target has been introduced for DBS in the treatment of some kinds of parkinsonism?
The newer target is the dentatorubrothalamic tract, where microelectrodes are introduced into the white fiber system for treating some kinds of parkinsonism.
What are the two methods mentioned for studying white fibers of the brain?

The white fibers of the brain can be studied in vitro by means of special anatomical techniques of dissection, or in vivo by specific sequences used in magnetic resonance, such as Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) tractography.
What is the centrum semiovale and where is it located?
The centrum semiovale is white matter with the shape of a semi-ovoid structure, recognized in the axial section of the hemispheres above the corpus callosum.
What are U-fibers and what is their function?
U-fibers are an example of association fibers that interconnect different regions of cortex within the same hemisphere, specifically around a sulcus.
Describe the corticospinal tract and its role in the pyramidal system.

The corticospinal tract is an exemplary projection system formed by axons of Betz's cells in the fifth cortical layer of the motor strip, constituting the pyramidal system. It contributes to the formation of the internal capsule.
What are the essential white fibers for identification in anatomical dissections of the brain?
The essential white fibers are the corpus callosum, internal capsule, corona radiata, anterior commissure, superior longitudinal fascicle, fornix, and uncinate fascicle.
What is the function of the uncinate fascicle, and what structure separates its leaves?
The uncinate fascicle is the main interconnection between the limbic and paralimbic structures, and its several leaves are separated by the gray matter of the claustrum.
What is the brain stem and what does it contain?

The brain stem is the intermediate structure between the spinal cord and the cerebellum and brain. It contains the main motor and sensory pathways of the nervous system, all the nuclei of ten cranial nerves (III-XII), and the main centers essential for life, such as the cardiorespiratory regulatory centers.
Where does the medulla oblongata begin and end?
The medulla oblongata begins at the foramen magnum as the direct continuation of the spinal cord and reaches the pons at the pontomedullary sulcus.
What are the three parts of the mesencephalon and what does each part contain?

The mesencephalon is formed by three parts: a dorsal part called the tectum (roof), where the four colliculi (quadrigeminal plate) are visible; the tegmentum; and the most ventral part formed by the cerebral peduncles (cerebral crus), containing the main descending tracts of the brain, and by the substantia nigra. The mesencephalon also contains the periaqueductal gray.
What is Wallenberg's syndrome and what causes it?

Wallenberg's syndrome, also called lateral medullary syndrome, is due to injury of the lateral part of the medulla, generally caused by ischemic occlusion of the vertebral artery or one of the cerebellar arteries.
Name four signs or symptoms of Wallenberg's syndrome and explain their anatomical basis.

Four signs/symptoms include: (1) vertigo, vomiting, nystagmus due to lesion of the vestibular nuclei; (2) pain and temperature sensation deficits in the contralateral side of the body due to lesion of the lateral spinothalamic tract; (3) pain and temperature sensation deficits in the ipsilateral side of the face due to lesion of the trigeminal system structures; (4) cerebellar dysfunction (dysdiadokokinesia, ataxia, dysmetria) on the ipsilateral side due to lesion of the inferior cerebellar peduncle.
What symptoms result from lesion of the connections between the nuclei of the IX and X nerves in Wallenberg's syndrome?
Lesion of the connections between the nuclei of the IX and X nerves results in dysphagia and hoarseness with deficits in the ipsilateral laryngeal, pharyngeal, and palatal territories of innervation.
What is Horner's syndrome in the context of Wallenberg's syndrome?
Horner's syndrome occurs in Wallenberg's syndrome due to lesion of the sympathetic fibers, leading to ptosis, myosis, and anhidrosis on the ipsilateral side of the body.
Why is the white matter of the cerebellum referred to as 'arbor vitae'?
The white matter has a geometric complexity resembling the arborizations of trees, which is why it is defined as arbor vitae.
What is the largest nuclear complex in the deep white matter of the cerebellum, and where does it project its fibers?
The largest nuclear complex is the dentate nucleus, which projects its fibers mainly to the contralateral red nucleus via the superior cerebellar peduncle.
What are the three pairs of cerebellar peduncles and their main connections?

The three pairs are: inferior cerebellar peduncle (connecting mainly to the spinal cord), middle cerebellar peduncle (connecting to the pons), and superior cerebellar peduncle (connecting to the red nucleus and thalamus).
What is the flocculonodular lobe, and what is its primary function?
The flocculonodular lobe is the anatomical name for the vestibulocerebellum, which is the phylogenetically oldest part (archicerebellum) and is mainly involved in the vestibular system.
Which part of the cerebellum is phylogenetically the most recent, and what functions is it involved in?
The cerebrocerebellum (formed by the cerebellar hemispheres) is the most recent (neocerebellum) and is involved in the control of sensory and motor functions, as well as cognitive functions.
What is the 'circle of Willis' and where is it located?

The 'circle of Willis' is an anastomotic arterial system that supports brain arterial circulation. It is located at the skull base, around the sellar region, and forms a network between the anterior circulation system (internal carotid arteries) and the posterior circulation system (vertebral arteries).
Which arteries support the anterior and posterior circulation systems?
The anterior circulation system is supported by the internal carotid arteries, and the posterior circulation system is supported by the vertebral arteries.
How does the origin of the common carotid artery differ between the right and left sides?
On the right side, the common carotid artery originates from the brachiocephalic trunk. On the left side, it originates directly from the aortic arch.
What is the function of the anterior communicating artery?

The anterior communicating artery is a small anastomotic artery that interconnects the two anterior cerebral arteries. It divides the anterior cerebral artery into a precommunicant segment (A1) and a postcommunicant segment (A2).
What was the accidental discovery made by Dr. Cooper in the 1950s regarding the anterior choroidal artery?

Dr. Cooper accidentally clipped the anterior choroidal artery during an operation for Parkinson's disease, causing ischemia of part of the globus pallidus. After the operation, the patient's tremor and rigidity improved without neurological deficits, likely due to anastomotic circulation from other arteries supplying the internal capsule.
How did the accidental discovery of clipping the anterior choroidal artery influence later treatments for Parkinson's disease?

The accidental discovery led to the temporary use of closing the anterior choroidal artery as a therapy for parkinsonism (though it is no longer used). It also prompted investigation into the role of the globus pallidus in extrapyramidal dysfunctions, which later became the surgical target for functional neurosurgical operations like deep brain stimulation.
Where do aneurysms commonly occur according to the content?
Aneurysms commonly occur at sites of hemodynamic turbulence, such as the bifurcation of the middle cerebral artery at the limen insulae or at the level of the anterior communicating artery.
What are the two main pathological conditions of brain arteries mentioned?
The two main pathological conditions are ischemic syndromes (due to occlusion) and hemorrhagic stroke (due to rupture, as in subarachnoid bleeding from aneurysms).
What are the two therapeutic techniques for occluding aneurysms described?

Endovascular techniques (introducing microcatheters from peripheral arteries to insert coils) and neurosurgical techniques (microneurosurgical intracisternal methods to place a clip on the aneurysm neck).
What characteristic of veins makes them more reliable anatomical markers of brain structures compared to arteries?
Veins are adherent to the brain while arteries pass through the cisterns, which makes them more reliable anatomical markers of brain structures and useful in neuroradiological imaging.
Into which two main groups can the veins of the brain be divided?
The veins of the brain can be divided into superficial veins, which drain mainly the cortex, and deep veins, which drain the deep parts of the brain.
Into which sinuses do the veins draining the cortical surface bring blood?
The veins draining the cortical surface bring blood to the superior and inferior sagittal sinuses, sphenoparietal sinus, cavernous sinus, and the sinuses around the tentorium.
What is the shape and location of the superior sagittal sinus?

The superior sagittal sinus is a triangular-shaped channel running medially in the groove of the lamina interna of the calvaria, from anteriorly behind the frontal sinus at the level of the foramen caecum to the confluence of the sinuses.
What are the lacunae adjacent to the superior sagittal sinus, and what do they collect?
The lacunae are venous spaces in the dura mater adjacent to the SSS that collect blood predominantly from the meningeal veins.
What are the three portions of the superior sagittal sinus?

The superior sagittal sinus is divided into an anterior third (from the foramen caecum to the coronal suture), a middle third (from the coronal suture to the lambdoid suture), and a posterior third (from the lambdoid suture to the confluens sinuum).
What is the clinical significance of occlusion of the posterior third portion of the superior sagittal sinus?
Occlusion of the posterior third portion of the superior sagittal sinus generally results in the death of the patient due to venous infarction of the brain.
Do cerebral veins and sinuses have valves? What does this imply?
Cerebral veins and sinuses lack valves, so the direction of blood flow is also dependent upon the position of the head.
Where does the inferior sagittal sinus run, and what veins does it predominantly drain?
The inferior sagittal sinus runs at the inferior border of the cerebral falx, above the corpus callosum, and drains blood collected predominantly by the pericallosal veins.
Where does the straight sinus originate, and what veins join it there?
The straight sinus originates behind the splenium of the corpus callosum, where the inferior sagittal sinus joins it together with the great deep veins, forming the vena magna (of Galen).
Which transverse sinus predominantly drains blood from the cortical surface, and which from the deep part of the brain?
The right transverse sinus predominantly drains blood coming from the cortical surface of the brain, while the left one predominantly drains blood coming from the deep part of the brain.
Why is the right transverse sinus and right jugular vein generally larger than the left?

The right transverse sinus and right jugular vein are generally larger because on the right side the jugular vein drains in a straight way (not in an angulated direction, as on the left side) into the subclavian vein.
Where is the cavernous sinus located, and from which structures does it drain blood?
The cavernous sinus is located laterally to the sella turcica and drains blood from the orbits (via the superior ophthalmic vein), sphenoparietal sinus, and superior and inferior petrosal sinuses.
What are the superior and inferior anastomotic veins, and which sinuses do they connect?

The superior anastomotic vein (vein of Trolard) connects the SSS with the sylvian veins, and the inferior anastomotic vein (vein of Labbé) connects the veins of the sylvian fissure with the transverse and sigmoid sinuses.
Which deep veins converge into the internal cerebral veins, basal veins, and vena magna?
The deep veins collect blood from deep brain structures and converge into the internal cerebral veins, basal veins (of Rosenthal), and vena magna (of Galen).
In the lateral ventricles, which veins can be observed quite constantly, and where do they converge?

In the lateral ventricles, the thalamostriate vein, choroidal vein, and vein of the septum pellucidum can be observed quite constantly, converging at the level of the interventricular foramen in the internal cerebral veins.
What is the velum interpositum?
The velum interpositum is a closed and thin space extending into the roof of the third ventricle from the pineal recess to the foramen of Monro.
Which veins collect blood from around the anterior perforated substance, and where do they join?
The basal veins (of Rosenthal) collect blood from around the anterior perforated substance and join posteriorly the internal cerebral veins and the vena magna.
What is cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, and what are some of its causes?
Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis is an occlusion of the veins occurring in diseases of coagulation, infections, or as a consequence of the use of drugs such as contraceptives.
What treatments are mentioned for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis?
The disease can be cured by anticoagulants, management of associated diseases such as intracranial hypertension and seizures, and endovascular thrombolysis.
What conditions was the pterional approach originally developed for?

It was developed for the microsurgical treatment of saccular aneurysms of the circle of Willis, frontoorbital and temporobasal arteriovenous malformations, cavernomas, and extrinsic and intrinsic tumors.
Why is the craniotomy called 'pterional'?

It is called 'pterional' because it is centered on the anatomical landmark 'pterion', the point of intersection of the sutures in the lateral cranial vault at the junction of the greater wing of the sphenoid, squamous temporal, frontal, and parietal bones.
Why is the reflection of the temporal muscle away from the bone a crucial point of the operation?
Because damage to the frontal branch of the facial nerve during this step can result in bad cosmetic and functional results for the patient.
What technique is used to save the frontal branch of the facial nerve?
The interfascial approach, which involves separating the galea aponeurotica from the pericranium between the layers of the superficial fascia temporal.
What bone prominences are drilled to improve the view of the surgical corridor?
Parts of the temporal squama, lesser wing sphenoid bones, and some prominences on the orbital roof (juga orbitales) can be drilled.
What does a small meningeal depression indicate on the dura mater?
It indicates the underlying zone of transition between the frontal lobe and the temporal lobe, where the lateral fissure is located.
What anatomical structures can be identified after dural opening?

The frontal and temporal lobes, arachnoid membrane over the lobes and bridging the sylvian fissure, arteries and veins, and sulci and gyri of the inferior frontal gyrus and superior temporal gyrus (e.g., pars orbitalis, pars triangularis, pars opercularis, superior temporal sulcus).
What are the main anatomical structures observed after opening the sylvian cistern?

Middle cerebral artery with its bifurcation at the limen insulae, internal carotid artery, anterior cerebral artery (A1 and A2), anterior communicating artery, lenticulostriate arteries, anterior choroidal artery, optic nerve, optic chiasm, olfactory nerve in the sulcus olfactorius, gyrus rectus, and oculomotor nerve.
What parts of the circle of Willis can be viewed through this approach?
It offers a wide view of the anterior part of the circle of Willis, and also the posterior part by extending the intracisternal approach more posteriorly.
What is the asterion and how is it used as a landmark in the retrosigmoid approach?

The asterion is the bone point where the lambdoid suture, parietomastoid suture, and occipitomastoid suture converge. It overlies the convergence of the transverse sinus with the sigmoid sinus, and inferior to it, a burr hole can be safely performed.
What is the primary objective of the interhemispheric transcallosal approach?
The primary objective is to access the corpus callosum and subsequently visualize the contents of the third ventricle through a small incision in the corpus callosum fibers.
Which arteries are visualized within the interhemispheric cistern during this approach?
The callosamarginal artery and the pericallosal artery (the continuation of the anterior cerebral artery over the corpus callosum) are visualized within the interhemispheric cistern.
What anatomical structures are retracted and incised to reach the third ventricle via this approach?

The medial surface of the frontal lobe is retracted laterally away from the cerebral falx, and a small incision (transversal or longitudinal) is made in the fibers of the corpus callosum to visualize the contents of the third ventricle.
What is the sphenoid sinus described as in the context of the endonasal transsphenoidal approach?

The sphenoid sinus is described as a very useful 'gait' to the whole central compartment of the skull base, from the crista galli to the clivus and anterior craniocervical junction, as well as to many regions of the brain such as the orbital gyri of the frontal lobes and the third ventricle.
What was the initial purpose of the endoscopic endonasal transsphenoidal approach?
The initial purpose was essentially for the treatment of pathologies of the sellar region, such as adenomas of the pituitary gland.
